questions.question: What are the four pathways through which the Social Cure framework explains how meaningful group membership produces mental health benefits?

options.text: Exercise, nutrition, sleep, and stress management
Belonging, identity, support, and purpose
Competition, achievement, recognition, and rewards
Attendance, participation, performance, and winning

questions.explanation: The Social Cure framework identifies four pathways: (1) Belonging — you have a place where you fit in, (2) Identity — shared sense of 'who we are', (3) Support — people notice and encourage you, and (4) Purpose — contributing to something larger than yourself. These are fundamental psychological needs. When group membership fulfills these needs, mental health improves. People with strong community belonging are 5.7 times more likely to report better mental health.

questions.question: According to the meta-analysis of 40 studies (n=14,462 athletes), how does social support correlate with wellbeing and mental health outcomes?

options.text: Social support shows no correlation with mental health
Positive correlation with wellbeing (r = 0.31); negative correlations with anxiety (r = −0.22), depression (r = −0.27), and stress (r = −0.25)
Social support only helps with physical health, not mental health
Too much social support actually worsens mental health

questions.explanation: Overall social support correlated positively with wellbeing (r = 0.31) and negatively with anxiety (r = −0.22), depression (r = −0.27), and stress (r = −0.25). Interestingly, family and friends' support showed STRONGER association with depressive symptoms than team-based support — meaning the team is not always the primary support source even in team sport contexts. This highlights that social support from multiple sources matters.

questions.question: Why do group exercise participants show stronger adherence than solo exercisers?

options.text: They are naturally more motivated people
Group exercisers arrive on time more consistently, are less likely to drop out, and show greater resilience to schedule disruptions due to social accountability and connection
Group exercise is easier than solo exercise
There is no difference in adherence between group and solo exercisers

questions.explanation: Group exercise participants show stronger adherence than solo exercisers — arriving on time more consistently, being less likely to drop out, and showing greater resilience to schedule disruptions. This adherence advantage comes from social accountability (people notice if you're absent), social connection (you enjoy seeing familiar faces), and fulfillment of psychological needs. This adherence advantage compounds over time: more consistent exercise exposure produces cumulative mental health benefit that solo exercisers may not achieve despite equivalent stated intention.

questions.question: What is the critical condition for social identity to produce mental health benefits according to the Social Cure research?

options.text: The group must be large (at least 50 members)
Social identity must be positive and meaningful; stigmatized or coercively controlled group memberships become a 'social curse' rather than a cure
Any group membership automatically produces benefits
The group must meet at least 5 times per week

questions.explanation: Social identity must be POSITIVE and MEANINGFUL to produce health benefits. When group membership is stigmatized, devalued, or coercively controlled, health benefits do NOT emerge — and may reverse, becoming a 'social curse' rather than a cure. This is why toxic team cultures, exclusionary groups, or forced memberships don't produce the mental health benefits. The quality of the group identity matters more than mere participation.

questions.question: Why is enjoyment considered both a mechanism AND a marker of whether collective activity will benefit mental health?

options.text: Enjoyment doesn't matter — discipline is more important
Enjoyment is a mechanism (positive emotions during activity contribute to wellbeing) AND a marker (if it's not enjoyable, the group probably isn't meeting psychological needs)
Enjoyment only matters for children, not adults
You should force yourself to continue even if you don't enjoy it

questions.explanation: Enjoyment is both a MECHANISM (positive emotions during the activity contribute directly to wellbeing) and a MARKER (if you consistently don't enjoy it, that signals the group probably isn't meeting your psychological needs for belonging, competence, or autonomy). Research consistently shows enjoyment is the #1 predictor of sustained participation and the #1 reason people quit when it's absent. If you're not enjoying a group activity, that's valuable information — not a sign you should force yourself to continue.

title: Mechanisms — How Collective Activity Works - Quiz
